sectionName: Biological and Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
A pharmacology lab characterizes the kinetics of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) using a synthetic peptide substrate. In the uninhibited condition, the enzyme exhibits Vmax = 100 μmol/min and Km = 5.0 mM. Two investigational inhibitors are tested separately at fixed concentration. Inhibitor X increases the apparent Km to 15.0 mM without changing Vmax. Inhibitor Y decreases the apparent Vmax to 50 μmol/min without changing Km. The results are shown in Figure 1.

The lab also performs Lineweaver-Burk analysis (1/v vs 1/[S]) for all three conditions. A clinical trial subsequently tests whether Inhibitor X lowers blood pressure in hypertensive patients compared to a standard ACE inhibitor. Researchers note that substrate concentration in vivo may differ substantially from the saturating conditions used in kinetic assays.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Michaelis-Menten plots showing reaction velocity as a function of substrate concentration for uninhibited ACE (solid), with competitive inhibitor X (dashed), and with noncompetitive inhibitor Y (dotted).
questions.stem: Based on Figure 1, Inhibitor X is best classified as which type of inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Competitive, because it increases Km without affecting Vmax

choices.label: B
choices.text: Noncompetitive, because it decreases Vmax without affecting Km

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uncompetitive, because it decreases both Km and Vmax proportionally

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mixed, because it affects both Km and Vmax to different degrees

explanations.A: Correct. Inhibitor X increases apparent Km from 5.0 to 15.0 mM while Vmax remains at 100 μmol/min. This is the hallmark of competitive inhibition: the inhibitor competes with substrate for the active site, requiring higher [S] to reach half-maximal velocity (increased Km). At saturating [S], the substrate outcompetes the inhibitor, so Vmax is unchanged. On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, competitive inhibition produces lines that intersect on the y-axis (same 1/Vmax).
explanations.B: This describes Inhibitor Y, not Inhibitor X. Noncompetitive inhibition decreases Vmax while Km remains unchanged because the inhibitor binds at an allosteric site regardless of substrate occupancy. Inhibitor X shows increased Km with unchanged Vmax, characteristic of competitive inhibition.
explanations.C: Uncompetitive inhibition involves the inhibitor binding only to the enzyme-substrate (ES) complex, decreasing both apparent Km and apparent Vmax. Inhibitor X increases Km without changing Vmax, which is the opposite of the uncompetitive pattern for Km.
explanations.D: Mixed inhibition affects both Km and Vmax because the inhibitor binds both free enzyme and ES complex with different affinities. Inhibitor X changes only Km while Vmax is preserved, which is the specific signature of pure competitive inhibition, not mixed.

questions.stem: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the line for the uninhibited enzyme and the line for Inhibitor Y would intersect at which location?

choices.label: A
choices.text: On the y-axis, because both have the same Vmax

choices.label: B
choices.text: They would be parallel lines with no intersection

choices.label: C
choices.text: At the origin, because both pass through zero

choices.label: D
choices.text: On the x-axis, because both have the same Km

explanations.A: Intersection on the y-axis means identical 1/Vmax values (same Vmax). Inhibitor Y decreases Vmax from 100 to 50 μmol/min, so the y-intercepts differ. Y-axis intersection is the Lineweaver-Burk hallmark of competitive inhibition, not noncompetitive.
explanations.B: Parallel lines on a Lineweaver-Burk plot indicate uncompetitive inhibition, where both Km and Vmax change proportionally so the slope (Km/Vmax) stays constant. Noncompetitive inhibition changes Vmax but not Km, altering the slope and producing intersecting, not parallel, lines.
explanations.C: Lineweaver-Burk plots (1/v vs 1/[S]) never pass through the origin because that would require infinite v at infinite [S], which contradicts enzyme saturation. Both axes represent reciprocal values, and all lines have positive y-intercepts and negative x-intercepts.
explanations.D: Correct. Inhibitor Y is noncompetitive (unchanged Km = 5.0 mM, decreased Vmax = 50). On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the x-intercept is –1/Km. Since both conditions share the same Km, their lines cross at x = –1/5.0 = –0.2 mM⁻¹. The y-intercepts differ (1/100 vs 1/50), producing lines that intersect on the x-axis—the diagnostic pattern for noncompetitive inhibition.

questions.stem: At a substrate concentration of 5.0 mM with Inhibitor X present, what is the reaction velocity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 25 μmol/min

choices.label: B
choices.text: 50 μmol/min

choices.label: C
choices.text: 75 μmol/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: 100 μmol/min

explanations.A: Correct. With Inhibitor X, Km(app) = 15.0 mM and Vmax = 100 μmol/min. Using Michaelis-Menten: v = Vmax·[S]/(Km + [S]) = 100 × 5.0/(15.0 + 5.0) = 500/20 = 25 μmol/min. At [S] = Km(app)/3, the enzyme is operating at only 25% of Vmax because substrate is well below the concentration needed for half-maximal velocity.
explanations.B: 50 μmol/min would be half of Vmax, which occurs when [S] = Km. With competitive inhibitor, Km(app) = 15.0 mM. Since [S] = 5.0 mM ≠ 15.0 mM, the velocity is not at Vmax/2. This answer confuses the original Km (5.0) with the apparent Km under inhibition.
explanations.C: 75 μmol/min corresponds to v at [S] = 3×Km for the uninhibited enzyme (5 × 3 = 15, giving 100 × 15/20 = 75). But with Inhibitor X, Km(app) = 15 mM, not 5 mM. Using the correct apparent Km in the Michaelis-Menten equation yields 25 μmol/min.
explanations.D: 100 μmol/min is Vmax, achieved only at saturating [S] (much greater than Km). At [S] = 5.0 mM with Km(app) = 15 mM, the enzyme is far from saturated and operates well below Vmax.

questions.stem: The researchers note that competitive inhibition can be overcome by increasing substrate concentration. At what [S] would the velocity with Inhibitor X equal 90% of Vmax?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 45 mM

choices.label: B
choices.text: 135 mM

choices.label: C
choices.text: 150 mM

choices.label: D
choices.text: 15 mM

explanations.A: At [S] = 45 mM: v = 100 × 45/(15 + 45) = 4500/60 = 75 μmol/min, which is 75% of Vmax. This is too low; 90% requires a higher substrate concentration to further outcompete the inhibitor.
explanations.B: Correct. For v = 0.9 × Vmax: 0.9 = [S]/(Km(app) + [S]). Solving: 0.9·Km(app) + 0.9[S] = [S], so 0.9 × 15 = 0.1[S], giving [S] = 135 mM. Verification: v = 100 × 135/(15 + 135) = 13500/150 = 90 μmol/min = 90% Vmax. Note this is 9× the apparent Km; for the uninhibited enzyme, 90% Vmax requires only 9 × 5 = 45 mM.
explanations.C: At [S] = 150 mM: v = 100 × 150/165 = 90.9 μmol/min ≈ 91% Vmax. This is close but slightly above the target. The exact answer is 135 mM, derived algebraically from the Michaelis-Menten equation set to 0.9×Vmax.
explanations.D: At [S] = Km(app) = 15 mM, v = Vmax/2 = 50 μmol/min = 50% of Vmax. Reaching 90% requires [S] = 9 × Km(app), far above the Km itself.

questions.stem: Which of the following would be expected if the enzyme followed an induced-fit model rather than the lock-and-key model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The enzyme active site would be rigid and complementary to the substrate in the unbound state

choices.label: B
choices.text: Substrate binding would not require any conformational change in the enzyme

choices.label: C
choices.text: The enzyme would undergo a conformational change upon substrate binding, optimizing catalytic geometry

choices.label: D
choices.text: The enzyme would catalyze any substrate with a similar molecular weight regardless of shape

explanations.A: A rigid, preformed active site describes the lock-and-key model proposed by Emil Fischer. In this model, the substrate fits perfectly into the active site without any conformational adjustment. The induced-fit model specifically rejects this rigid complementarity.
explanations.B: The absence of conformational change also describes the lock-and-key model. The induced-fit model proposed by Koshland holds that the active site is not perfectly complementary until the substrate begins to bind, triggering a conformational change that shapes the active site around the substrate.
explanations.C: Correct. In the induced-fit model (Koshland, 1958), substrate binding induces a conformational change in the enzyme that brings catalytic residues into optimal alignment. The active site molds around the substrate, achieving complementary geometry only upon binding. This model explains why many enzymes show measurable conformational changes (detectable by X-ray crystallography) between bound and unbound states and why some near-substrates bind but are not catalyzed.
explanations.D: Neither model predicts catalysis based solely on molecular weight. Enzyme specificity depends on the chemical and geometric complementarity between substrate and active site, not mass. The induced-fit model actually predicts high specificity because only the correct substrate induces the precise conformational change needed for catalysis.

questions.passage:
A research group studies lysozyme, an enzyme that cleaves the β-1,4-glycosidic bond in bacterial peptidoglycan. They measure initial velocities at varying substrate concentrations under several conditions: standard (37°C, pH 5.0), elevated temperature (50°C, pH 5.0), and suboptimal pH (37°C, pH 8.0). At 50°C, the enzyme shows higher initial velocity for the first 5 minutes but declining activity thereafter. At pH 8.0, activity drops to 30% of the pH 5.0 rate.

The group also tests a modified substrate analog that binds the active site with higher affinity than the natural substrate but cannot undergo cleavage. When this analog is added at equimolar concentration with the natural substrate, the initial velocity drops by 50%. A second experiment introduces a covalent modifier that reacts with a cysteine residue distant from the active site, reducing Vmax by 40% without affecting Km.
questions.stem: The declining activity at 50°C after 5 minutes is most likely caused by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: substrate depletion as the faster reaction consumes available peptidoglycan

choices.label: B
choices.text: product inhibition from accumulating N-acetylmuramic acid fragments

choices.label: C
choices.text: thermal denaturation disrupting the enzyme's tertiary structure and active site geometry

choices.label: D
choices.text: a shift in the equilibrium constant making the reverse reaction thermodynamically favorable

explanations.A: If substrate were simply being consumed, the initial velocity would decrease gradually from the start, not show a characteristic pattern of initial increase followed by decline. Additionally, the passage specifies varying substrate concentrations in the experimental design, and the decline occurs within 5 minutes regardless of initial [S], consistent with enzyme inactivation rather than substrate limitation.
explanations.B: Product inhibition would reduce velocity progressively as products accumulate, but the pattern described—higher initial velocity followed by declining activity—is characteristic of irreversible enzyme inactivation. Product inhibition is typically reversible and would not produce the sharp activity decline observed at elevated temperature.
explanations.C: Correct. At 50°C, thermal energy initially increases molecular collisions and reaction rate (higher initial velocity per the Arrhenius equation). However, temperatures above the enzyme's optimal range progressively disrupt noncovalent interactions (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, van der Waals forces) that maintain tertiary structure. As the enzyme denatures, the active site geometry is lost irreversibly, explaining the decline in activity over time despite initial rate enhancement.
explanations.D: Enzyme-catalyzed reactions reach the same equilibrium regardless of enzyme presence—enzymes affect only the rate. Temperature does shift equilibrium constants, but for hydrolysis reactions like glycosidic bond cleavage, the forward reaction remains highly favorable. The activity decline reflects enzyme inactivation, not a thermodynamic shift.

questions.stem: The substrate analog that binds but cannot be cleaved functions as which type of inhibitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Noncompetitive inhibitor, because it binds at an allosteric site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Uncompetitive inhibitor, because it binds only to the ES complex

choices.label: C
choices.text: Irreversible inhibitor, because it permanently modifies the active site

choices.label: D
choices.text: Competitive inhibitor, because it occupies the active site and blocks substrate binding

explanations.A: Noncompetitive inhibitors bind at sites other than the active site. The passage states this analog binds the active site itself with high affinity. Active site occupancy by a non-cleavable analog is the defining feature of competitive, not allosteric, inhibition.
explanations.B: Uncompetitive inhibitors bind only the enzyme-substrate complex (ES), not free enzyme. A substrate analog that occupies the active site competes with substrate for binding to the free enzyme. Additionally, uncompetitive inhibition decreases both apparent Km and Vmax, which is not the expected pattern here.
explanations.C: Irreversible inhibitors form covalent bonds with the enzyme, permanently inactivating it. The passage describes a substrate analog that binds with high affinity but does not mention covalent modification. High-affinity noncovalent binding is reversible, even if the dissociation rate is slow. Competitive inhibitors can have very low Ki values without being irreversible.
explanations.D: Correct. The analog binds the active site (same binding location as the substrate) and cannot be cleaved, so it blocks access of the natural substrate to the active site. This is classic competitive inhibition. Because the inhibitor is a substrate analog, increasing the natural substrate concentration can partially overcome its effect, although the exact fraction of enzyme bound to inhibitor depends on relative concentrations and binding affinities.

questions.stem: The covalent modifier that reacts with a distant cysteine residue, reducing Vmax by 40% without affecting Km, most likely inhibits the enzyme by which mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Competitive inhibition at a secondary substrate binding site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allosteric modulation that alters the enzyme's catalytic conformation without affecting substrate affinity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uncompetitive inhibition through binding to the enzyme-substrate complex

choices.label: D
choices.text: Feedback inhibition by the reaction product

explanations.A: Competitive inhibition requires binding at the substrate binding site and produces increased apparent Km with unchanged Vmax. The covalent modifier binds a distant cysteine (not the active site) and changes Vmax without affecting Km—the opposite of competitive inhibition kinetics.
explanations.B: Correct. The modifier covalently reacts with a cysteine distant from the active site, so it does not compete with substrate. The unchanged Km indicates that substrate binding affinity is unaffected, while the reduced Vmax indicates impaired catalytic turnover. This is consistent with an allosteric conformational change that distorts the catalytic machinery without blocking substrate access—a hallmark of noncompetitive or mixed-type inhibition via covalent modification at a regulatory site.
explanations.C: Uncompetitive inhibition requires binding specifically to the ES complex and characteristically decreases both apparent Km and Vmax proportionally. The passage states Km is unchanged while Vmax decreases, which is inconsistent with uncompetitive kinetics.
explanations.D: Feedback inhibition is a regulatory mechanism involving downstream metabolic products, not a covalent chemical modifier. Additionally, the passage identifies the modifier as a synthetic reagent reacting with a cysteine residue, not as a product of the lysozyme reaction.

questions.stem: At pH 8.0, lysozyme activity drops to 30% of the pH 5.0 rate. This is most likely because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: hydroxide ions competitively inhibit the enzyme by binding to the active site

choices.label: B
choices.text: the substrate peptidoglycan undergoes spontaneous hydrolysis at alkaline pH, leaving no substrate for the enzyme

choices.label: C
choices.text: the enzyme denatures irreversibly above pH 7.0 due to disulfide bond reduction

choices.label: D
choices.text: key active site residues such as glutamic acid (pKa ≈ 4.1) and aspartic acid (pKa ≈ 3.9) lose their protonation states needed for catalysis

explanations.A: Hydroxide ions do not function as competitive inhibitors of lysozyme. The active site binds a large oligosaccharide substrate through multiple contacts; a small hydroxide ion would not effectively compete for this binding site. The activity reduction reflects changes in the protonation states of catalytic residues, not inhibitor binding.
explanations.B: Peptidoglycan is chemically stable at pH 8.0; spontaneous hydrolysis of glycosidic bonds requires much more extreme conditions (concentrated acid or prolonged heating). The substrate remains intact at mildly alkaline pH. The activity loss reflects enzyme rather than substrate chemistry.
explanations.C: Lysozyme is notably stable across a wide pH range and does not denature at pH 8.0. Its four disulfide bonds contribute to structural stability and are not reduced at mildly alkaline pH. Denaturation would require much harsher conditions (pH >11 or high temperature). The activity loss is due to altered ionization of catalytic residues, not structural unfolding.
explanations.D: Correct. Lysozyme's catalytic mechanism depends on two key residues: Glu35 (acting as a general acid, donating a proton) and Asp52 (stabilizing the oxocarbenium ion intermediate). At pH 5.0, Glu35 is protonated (pKa ≈ 6.5 in the enzyme microenvironment) and can donate a proton. At pH 8.0, Glu35 becomes deprotonated and can no longer function as a proton donor, drastically reducing catalytic efficiency. The pH-activity profile of lysozyme shows optimal activity near pH 5.0 because this is where the required protonation states of both catalytic residues are simultaneously satisfied.

questions.stem: If the catalytic rate constant (kcat) is 150 s⁻¹ and the Km is 5.0 mM, what is the catalytic efficiency (kcat/Km), and what does it indicate about the enzyme?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 3.0 × 10⁴ M⁻¹s⁻¹; the enzyme operates well below the diffusion limit

choices.label: B
choices.text: 750 mM/s; the enzyme is operating at maximum possible speed

choices.label: C
choices.text: 30 s⁻¹; the enzyme has reached catalytic perfection

choices.label: D
choices.text: 3.0 × 10⁴ M⁻¹s⁻¹; the enzyme has achieved diffusion-limited kinetics

explanations.A: Correct. kcat/Km = 150 s⁻¹ / (5.0 × 10⁻³ M) = 3.0 × 10⁴ M⁻¹s⁻¹. This is a useful measure of catalytic efficiency at low [S]. The diffusion limit for enzyme catalysis is approximately 10⁸–10⁹ M⁻¹s⁻¹, so at 3.0 × 10⁴, lysozyme operates about 10,000-fold below diffusion-limited perfection. Enzymes like carbonic anhydrase and superoxide dismutase approach the diffusion limit; lysozyme does not.
explanations.B: This answer incorrectly multiplies kcat by Km rather than dividing. Additionally, the units (mM/s) are incorrect for catalytic efficiency, which should be M⁻¹s⁻¹. Catalytic efficiency is always kcat divided by Km, yielding a second-order rate constant.
explanations.C: Dividing kcat by Km expressed in mM without unit conversion gives 150/5 = 30, but the units (s⁻¹) are wrong. Proper unit conversion (mM to M) is essential: 5.0 mM = 5.0 × 10⁻³ M. Catalytic perfection (diffusion limit) requires kcat/Km ≈ 10⁸–10⁹ M⁻¹s⁻¹, far above the calculated value.
explanations.D: The kcat/Km calculation is correct (3.0 × 10⁴ M⁻¹s⁻¹), but the interpretation is wrong. Diffusion-limited kinetics requires kcat/Km ≈ 10⁸–10⁹ M⁻¹s⁻¹. At 3.0 × 10⁴, the enzyme is approximately four orders of magnitude below the diffusion limit. Enzymes at the diffusion limit include triose phosphate isomerase and acetylcholinesterase.
